skill.name: azure-ai-vision-imageanalysis-py
skill.description: Azure AI Vision Image Analysis SDK guidance for Python developers. Covers captions, tags, objects, OCR, people detection, smart crops, authentication, and error handling.
skill.summary: Build Python image analysis workflows with Azure AI Vision features and authentication patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer heavily over-reported Markdown code fences as Ruby or shell backtick execution, and the weak-crypto and reconnaissance hits are false positives. The skill is still medium risk because it documents installing an external package, reading Azure credentials from environment variables, and sending image data or image URLs to Azure AI Vision.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Image Analysis Sends Data to Azure
medium_findings.description: The examples configure an Azure Cognitive Services endpoint and call Azure AI Vision analysis methods for URLs and local image bytes. This is expected behavior for the skill, but users may send sensitive images or image URLs to a cloud service if they run the examples without reviewing data handling requirements.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit in the Azure endpoint and analyze_from_url/analyze examples. It appears legitimate for an Azure Vision SDK guide, with no evidence of hidden or unauthorized destinations.

medium_findings.title: Credential Access Through Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: The examples read VISION_ENDPOINT and VISION_KEY from environment variables and also show DefaultAzureCredential. This is a standard Azure pattern, but it requires users to protect API keys and avoid exposing credentials in logs, prompts, or shared files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential reads are direct and visible in Python examples. The usage is normal for Azure SDK authentication, but credentials are security-sensitive by nature.

medium_findings.title: External Package Installation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install azure-ai-vision-imageanalysis with pip. This is a normal setup step, but it introduces supply-chain and dependency trust considerations for users who run the command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pip install command is clear and limited to the declared Azure Vision package. The risk is dependency-related rather than evidence of malicious command execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings point to Markdown code fences, inline code, and documentation examples. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or hidden script execution in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown fences and Python or bash examples, not executable Ruby backtick syntax. The only actual shell instruction is the visible pip install example.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings do not correspond to cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or certificate handling. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata, a Markdown table, and a best-practice sentence. They do not contain cryptographic operations or algorithm names.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding points to a best-practice line about using async clients for high-throughput scenarios. No evidence found of host probing, OS enumeration, environment dumping, or system discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain documentation text about performance. It does not request or execute any system reconnaissance activity.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined code execution, network, and credential access signals. Review found visible SDK examples for Azure authentication and image analysis, but no obfuscation, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden malicious workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk factors exist in documentation examples, but the context supports the advertised Azure Vision purpose. No suspicious instructions, disguised endpoints, or prompt-injection text were found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Enabled SDK Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use Azure AI Vision client methods that contact a configured Azure endpoint. This is required for the SDK, but users should review privacy, retention, and compliance requirements before analyzing sensitive images.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The client endpoint and analysis calls are explicit. The pattern is expected for cloud image analysis and not hidden.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Based Secret Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples load Azure endpoint and key values from environment variables. This avoids hardcoding secrets, but it still handles credentials that must be protected.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly accesses os.environ for Azure configuration. This is a common secure pattern when the environment is managed correctly.

content.user_title: Analyze Images with Azure AI Vision
content.value_statement: Developers need reliable examples for Azure AI Vision image analysis in Python. This skill provides concise patterns for captions, OCR, object detection, people detection, smart crops, authentication, and error handling.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and import the Azure AI Vision Image Analysis SDK for Python.
Demonstrates API key and Entra ID authentication patterns for Azure Vision clients.
Explains image analysis from URLs and local image bytes.
Covers captions, dense captions, tags, objects, OCR, people detection, and smart crops.
Includes async client usage, supported image requirements, and HttpResponseError handling.
limitations: Does not create Azure resources or manage Azure subscriptions.
Requires a valid Azure AI Vision endpoint and credentials before examples can run.
Does not provide a complete application, user interface, or deployment workflow.
Image analysis results depend on Azure service availability, model behavior, and image quality.

use_cases.title: Prototype Image Understanding
use_cases.description: Build a quick Python proof of concept that describes images, extracts tags, and reads visible text.

use_cases.title: Prepare Media Metadata
use_cases.description: Generate captions, tags, crop suggestions, and object locations for an image library or content pipeline.

use_cases.title: Add OCR to Workflows
use_cases.description: Extract text and word confidence details from uploaded images for review or downstream processing.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Caption Example
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single image URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use this skill to generate a caption for one image URL with Azure AI Vision in Python.

prompt_templates.title: OCR and Tags Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt the examples to extract OCR text and tags from a local image file using my Azure Vision endpoint.

prompt_templates.title: Production Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a robust Python workflow based on this skill that handles Azure authentication, HttpResponseError, and invalid image input.

prompt_templates.title: Async Batch Analysis Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an async batch image analysis approach using this skill, with feature selection, retry guidance, and cost-aware recommendations.

output_examples.input: Analyze a product photo from a URL for captions and tags.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan that authenticates to Azure Vision, requests caption and tag features, and reports confidence values.

output_examples.input: Extract text from a scanned receipt image.
output_examples.output: A Python-oriented workflow that sends local image bytes to Azure Vision, reads line and word results, and handles service errors.

output_examples.input: Suggest thumbnail crops for a media library.
output_examples.output: Guidance for requesting smart crop regions with target aspect ratios and storing crop coordinates with image metadata.

best_practices: Request only the visual features you need to control latency and Azure costs.
Use managed identity or DefaultAzureCredential when possible instead of long-lived API keys.
Review data handling rules before sending private images or URLs to a cloud vision service.
anti_patterns: Do not paste Azure keys into prompts, source files, notebooks, or shared logs.
Do not analyze sensitive images unless your Azure configuration and compliance requirements allow it.
Do not request every visual feature by default when a narrower request will solve the task.

faq.question: Does this skill run image analysis by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides Python SDK guidance and examples that users can adapt in their own environment.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: You need an Azure AI Vision endpoint and either an API key or Entra ID authentication.

faq.question: Can it analyze local image files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows how to read local image bytes and send them to the Azure analysis client.

faq.question: Which visual features are covered?
faq.answer: It covers captions, dense captions, tags, object detection, OCR, people detection, and smart crops.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. It uses normal Azure SDK patterns but involves cloud calls and credentials.

faq.question: Does it include deployment guidance?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on SDK usage patterns, authentication examples, feature selection, and error handling.
